name: Rodén and Salada de Mediana Loop
name_original: Antiguo pueblo de Rodén y Salada de la Mediana — circular desde Mediana de Aragón
description: This difficult 19.1-mile touring cycling route from Mediana de Aragón explores the historic Old village of Rodén and the unique Salada de Me
region_intro: Touring cycling around Mediana De Aragón offers routes through a semi-arid steppe environment, contrasting with the lush riparian ecosystems along the Ebro River. The landscape features gently rolling hills, cultivated fields, and unique natural formations like the Salada de Mediana salt flat. Cyclists can explore historical engineering marvels such as the Canal Imperial de Aragón, which provides a blend of natural beauty and historical infrastructure. The region's diverse terrain includes both flat sections along waterways and more challenging climbs in the rolling hills. Best touring cycling routes around Mediana De Aragón The most popular touring cycling route is Rodén and Salada de Mediana Loop, a 19.1 miles (30.7 km) trail that takes 2 hours 8 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the historic Old Village of Rodén and the unique Salada de Mediana salt flat. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Valdegurriana Locks – Bicentennial Footbridge loop from El Burgo de Ebro, a moderate 22.2 miles (35.7 km) path. This route follows sections of the historic Canal Imperial de Aragón, showcasing its impressive locks and aqueducts. Local touring cyclists also love the Galacho de El Burgo de Ebro – Camino La Mejana loop from El Burgo de Ebro, a 13.7 miles (22.1 km) trail leading through the natural reserve of groves and galachos of the Ebro, often completed in about 1 hour 55 minutes. Touring cycling around Mediana De Aragón is defined by semi-arid steppe landscapes, the Ebro River valley, and the historical Canal Imperial de Aragón. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy riverside paths to more difficult routes with greater elevation gain. The routes in Mediana De Aragón are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 100 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Mediana De Aragón's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Mediana De Aragón?
A: There are over 5 touring cycling routes documented on komoot around Mediana De Aragón, offering diverse options for exploring the region's unique landscapes.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while touring cycling in Mediana De Aragón?
A: The area features a distinct semi-arid steppe environment, which contrasts with the lush riparian ecosystems found along the Ebro River. You'll cycle through gently rolling hills, cultivated fields, and encounter unique natural formations like the Salada de Mediana salt flat.

Q: Are there routes suitable for different cycling skill levels?
A: Yes, the routes around Mediana De Aragón cater to various abilities. While there are 2 difficult routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993395116/roden-and-salada-de-mediana-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rodén and Salada de Mediana Loop&lt;/a&gt;, there are also 3 moderate and 1 easy route, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38143485" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Galacho de El Burgo de Ebro – Camino La Mejana loop from El Burgo de Ebro&lt;/a&gt;, offering options for most touring cyclists.

Q: What unique natural features can I explore on a touring bike?
A: You can visit the striking &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5146890" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Salada de Mediana Salt Flat&lt;/a&gt;, a seasonal endorheic lake known for its white salt layer when dry. Another highlight is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5631455" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Galacho de El Burgo de Ebro&lt;/a&gt;, an oxbow lake showcasing the Ebro River's dynamic history. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993395116/roden-and-salada-de-mediana-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rodén and Salada de Mediana Loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically takes you past the salt flat.

Q: Are there any historical sites or landmarks along the touring cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. You can explore the historic Old Village of Rodén, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5146908" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iron Age settlement at Cabezo de Rodén&lt;/a&gt; offers panoramic views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20743113" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdegurriana Locks – Bicentennial Footbridge loop from El Burgo de Ebro&lt;/a&gt; follows sections of the historical Canal Imperial de Aragón, an 18th-century engineering marvel with impressive locks and aqueducts.

Q: Are there any routes that offer good viewpoints?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2080272" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Verde Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, located northeast of the Las Planas wind farm area, provides beautiful views towards Torrecilla de Valmadrid. While not directly on a touring route listed, it's a popular spot in the wider area for scenic vistas.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Mediana De Aragón?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from flat riverside paths to more challenging routes with elevation gain, and the blend of natural beauty with historical infrastructure like the Canal Imperial de Aragón.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993395116/roden-and-salada-de-mediana-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rodén and Salada de Mediana Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20743113" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdegurriana Locks – Bicentennial Footbridge loop from El Burgo de Ebro&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Mediana De Aragón?
A: Given the semi-arid steppe environment, spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) are generally the most pleasant times for touring cycling. Temperatures are milder, and the landscapes are often at their most vibrant, avoiding the intense heat of summer.

Q: How long do the touring cycling routes typically take to complete?
A: Route durations vary depending on distance and difficulty. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38143485" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Galacho de El Burgo de Ebro – Camino La Mejana loop from El Burgo de Ebro&lt;/a&gt; (22.1 km) takes about 1 hour 55 minutes, while the more challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20143744" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Foz de Zafrane – View of Casa Perera loop from Valmadrid&lt;/a&gt; (34.7 km) can take around 3 hours 12 minutes.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes that follow the Ebro River?
A: Yes, the region's proximity to the Ebro River means you can find routes that explore its valley and riparian ecosystems. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38143485" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Galacho de El Burgo de Ebro – Camino La Mejana loop from El Burgo de Ebro&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, leads through the natural reserve of groves and galachos of the Ebro, showcasing the river's influence on the landscape.

Q: Can I bring my family on these touring cycling routes?
A: Many routes are suitable for families, especially those with easier difficulty grades and flatter sections along waterways. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38143485" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Galacho de El Burgo de Ebro – Camino La Mejana loop from El Burgo de Ebro&lt;/a&gt;, being an easy route, could be a good option for a family outing.

faqs.question: How difficult is this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as difficult. While sections in the Ebro Valley might be relatively flat, you should expect rolling hills and potential ascents, especially towards viewpoints like Cabezo de Rodén. Some parts might be unpaved, requiring good physical fitness and appropriate cycling skills. It's best suited for experienced touring cyclists.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain will I encounter on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is diverse, characteristic of the Ebro Valley and surrounding areas in Aragon. You'll cycle through plains and rolling hills, with potential unpaved sections. The route features the unique environment of a 'salada' (saline lake) and areas with historical significance, offering a varied scenic experience.

faqs.question: What historical and natural landmarks can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter a rich blend of history and nature. Key historical sites include the Church of Rodén, the El Cabezo de la Horca (an Iron Age settlement), and Rodén Castle, showcasing the region's Mudéjar heritage. For natural beauty, you'll pass by the unique Salada de Mediana, a saline lake, and enjoy panoramic views from elevated points.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle in Aragon, including this route, is typically during the spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) when temperatures are milder and the landscape is often at its most vibrant. Summers can be very hot, especially in the Ebro Valley, and winters can be cold.

faqs.question: Where can I park my car to access this circular route?
faqs.answer: As this is a circular route starting from Mediana de Aragón, you can typically find parking within the village itself. Look for public parking areas or street parking that allows for longer stays. Always check local signage for any restrictions.

faqs.question: Is this touring bicycle route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While many rural areas in Aragon are generally welcoming, specific rules for dogs on cycling routes can vary. Given the presence of historical sites and potentially sensitive natural areas like the Salada de Mediana, it's advisable to keep dogs on a leash at all times. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved and that you clean up after them.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are generally required for cycling this public route. However, if you plan to visit any specific historical sites or attractions along the way, they might have their own entry fees or guided tour costs.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other established paths. You will find yourself on parts of the Camino del Maestrazgo-Bajo Aragón, the Camino de la salada, and the Camino Jacobeo del Ebro, among others, for varying distances.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or get refreshments near the route?
faqs.answer: Mediana de Aragón, as the starting and end point, is the most reliable place to find cafes, restaurants, or shops for refreshments before or after your ride. Along the route, especially in more rural sections, options may be limited, so it's best to carry sufficient water and snacks.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' rating, varied terrain, and potential unpaved sections, this specific circular route is generally not recommended for families with young children or beginner cyclists. It's better suited for experienced touring cyclists looking for a challenging ride.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a touring bicycle trip on this route?
faqs.answer: For this route, you should bring a well-maintained touring bicycle suitable for varied terrain, a helmet, sufficient water (especially on hot days), snacks, sun protection (sunscreen, hat), basic bike repair tools, a first-aid kit, and a fully charged phone with the Komoot app for navigation. Layers of clothing are advisable due to potential temperature changes.
